Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I work part-time and have a lot going on at home. I tried to carve out chunks of time, usually late at night or early morning before anyone else was up. I'd make visual study guides and color-coded notes since that's how things stick for me. I aimed for about 23 hours a week, but it wasn't always in neat blocks—sometimes it was stolen moments between shifts or family responsibilities.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life outside school. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, studying the same times every day, but then something would come up—extra work shifts, family stuff—and everything would shift. I learned to be flexible because rigid schedules just don't work with my situation. Deadlines definitely pushed me to reorganize my whole week when they were approaching.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check what assignments were coming up or to watch lecture videos I'd missed. I couldn't always make it to class—my attendance was pretty rough this semester—so the LMS was my lifeline for catching up. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or if classmates had posted something helpful in the discussions. It became my main connection to the course content.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS kind of became automatic for me, like checking my phone in the morning. I'd log in during my breaks at work or right before bed to see what I needed to tackle. It happened naturally because I knew I was missing classes and needed to stay on top of things somehow. The discussions were actually engaging enough that I wanted to check in and see what people were saying.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm a visual learner and I missed so many classes. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to make my own diagrams and visual summaries. The discussion forums were also really valuable because interacting with my peers helped me understand concepts from different angles. I posted a lot and responded to others because talking through ideas helps cement them for me.
Q6: Some of the dense reading materials I'd skim or put off because walls of text just don't work for my brain. I learn better from videos, diagrams, and interactive stuff, so long PDFs often got downloaded but barely touched. Some supplementary resources felt redundant if the videos already covered the material well. With limited time, I had to prioritize what actually helped me learn versus what was just extra.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. Visual content, interactive elements, or discussions where I could engage with real people—those felt valuable. If something felt like busywork or repetitive, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with my schedule, but sometimes they were actually more interesting because there was no pressure. Required ones I'd power through even if I was bored because I knew my grade depended on it. The optional stuff I'd only do if it genuinely seemed helpful or if I had extra time, which was rare. Sometimes the optional discussions were more engaging than the required readings.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a ton of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs to study during my commute or at work when there was downtime. I made physical flashcards and visual mind maps that I'd review constantly, none of which showed up anywhere in the system. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well enough for me.
Q10: There were so many times I was deep in studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out slides and annotate them by hand for hours. I'd be quizzing myself with flashcards on the bus or explaining concepts out loud to my younger sister to test if I really understood them. My best studying often happened away from screens, working through problems in a notebook or creating visual summaries on poster paper.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, then download it and rewatch it multiple times offline. Same with readings—I'd access them once to download, then study from the PDF on my phone or printed copies. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so downloading everything became a survival strategy. The LMS would only show that one initial access even though I engaged with the material repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when work scheduled me extra shifts and I was exhausted. I'd feel bored and disconnected from the material, just going through the motions. Family responsibilities piled up too, and some weeks I barely had energy to look at coursework. But I kept pushing through because I've overcome a lot to get to university and I wasn't about to give up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly—sometimes assignments for other classes or urgent family stuff took priority. Confusion with certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily until I had mental energy to tackle them. The mindfulness exercises actually helped me manage the stress better.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open while doing other things, or I'd be watching a video but my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or work. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. There were times I'd click through materials quickly just to mark them as complete, planning to come back later when I could actually focus. Being present physically isn't the same as being mentally engaged.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on exam prep, which probably explains my exam score even though my assignments were strong. Exams stress me out and I'd avoid studying for them by focusing on assignments instead—at least those felt more controllable. Sometimes I'd put off confusing topics hoping they'd make more sense later, which didn't always work out. I knew it was important but the anxiety around exams made me avoid them until the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines basically ran my life this semester. I'd organize my entire week around what was due and when, often working backward from due dates. The LMS became most active for me in the days leading up to submissions—that's when I'd be watching videos, checking discussions, and finalizing work. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly, but honestly they kept me accountable when everything else was chaotic.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was positive—seeing good scores on assignments made me feel like my hard work was paying off. When I got constructive criticism, I'd try to apply it to the next assignment, which kept me engaged with the course materials. The problem was feedback on exams came too late to really help me adjust my study strategies. I wish there had been more low-stakes feedback opportunities throughout.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within, which I needed given my chaotic schedule. But the pacing sometimes didn't match my life—some weeks were lighter when I had time, and heavy content dropped when I was swamped with work. The discussion requirements actually kept me connected to the class even when I couldn't attend in person.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn visually and on my own schedule. Having discussion forums where I could interact with peers helped me feel less isolated despite my low attendance. What made it harder was when materials assumed you'd been in class or when there were no visual aids for complex concepts. The exam format was tough because it didn't play to my strengths the way assignments did.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality work, or when life circumstances prevent them from completing submissions even though they're engaging with content. It could also be someone who's confused and keeps revisiting materials without making progress. Or maybe they're perfectionists who keep reviewing but never feel ready to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually resonates with me in a way—my attendance was really low but I was still learning, just not always through the LMS. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours online, or they study effectively offline. They might have strong background knowledge or learn well from textbooks and external resources. The LMS doesn't capture everything, so low activity there doesn't mean low engagement overall.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just clicking through. They can't see offline studying, physical notes, group study sessions, or the mental work of processing information. They definitely can't capture motivation, understanding, or the personal circumstances affecting your engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting concepts to real life, and wanting to understand deeply. For me, engagement looks like creating visual summaries, discussing ideas with peers, and applying what I learn. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me—the financial stress, work schedule, family obligations. It can't capture the hours I spent studying on buses, during work breaks, or late at night after everyone was asleep. It doesn't show my resilience, how I picked myself up after disappointing exam scores and kept pushing forward. My journey is so much more than login timestamps and click data.
